coredata.dc:title: Sandwich-like electron transporting layer to achieve highly efficient perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: He, Li
Lv, Zheng
Jiang, Haipeng
Ma, Xiaojun
Wang, Fengyou
Fan, Lin
Wei, Maobin
Yang, Jinghai
Yang, Lili
Liu, Huilian
coredata.dc:description: Passivating the carrier recombination at the heterojunction interface and improving the efficiency of charge separation are effective means to boost the performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The interface modification between the anode and the electron transporting layer (ETL) or constructing bilayer structural ETLs has been proved to be the effective way to achieve high-efficient charge extraction and collection. Combining the advantages of both techniques might further achieve lower energy loss and higher efficiency in PSCs. Herein, we design a sandwich-like SnO2-CQDs-SnO2 (S–C–S) ETLs, i.e. an ultrathin band-gap tunable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) layer is inserted between ultrathin SnO2 bottom layer and SnO2 top layer. The bottom ultrathin SnO2 layer passivates the defects of SnO2:F (FTO) and reduces the carrier recombination at the FTO/ETLs interface. The CQDs layer enhances the optical transmission of ETLs, accelerates carrier transport process and improves the hole-blocking ability. Such S–C–S ETLs greatly enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs and eliminate hysteresis to the maximum extent. This work provides a new concept for designing novel electronic transmission materials for solar cells, and lays the foundation for further achieving higher PCE in PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cell
Sandwich-like electron transporting layer
Interface modification
Band alignment
Power conversion efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme image of synthetic process of nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CQDs.

(a) A typical TEM image of CQDs. The upper inset represents the HRTEM image of CQDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs in water. The inset image is the photograph of water solution of CQDs. (c) PL spectra of CQDs in water by different excitation wavelengths.

(a) XPS spectra of N 1s peaks of FTO/SnO2 and FTO/SnO2/CQDs samples. (b) Transmittance spectra of pristine FTO, FTO/SnO2 and FTO/S–C–S. AFM images and RMS parameters of (c) bare FTO; (d) FTO/SnO2; (e) FTO/SnO2/CQDs; (f) FTO/S–C–S.
Top-view SEM images of (a) FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and (b) FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3; (c) XRD patterns of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3; (d) Optical absorption spectra of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3.

(a) The schematic representation of S–C–S based PSCs. (b) Cross-sectional SEM image of S–C–S based PSCs. (c) J-V curves of optimized SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs with both reverse and forward scans. (d) Histogram of the PCE values for SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs. (e) EQE spectra and Jint of SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs. (f) The energy band diagram of S–C–S based PSCs.

(a) The perovskite can directly contact the FTO surface along a shunt pathway with pinholes. (b) The ultrathin SnO2 and CQDs can inhibit the penetration of perovskite reaching the FTO surface. (c) Steady state PL spectra and (d) TRPL spectra of FTO/SnO2 (S–C–S)/MAPbI3.

(a) J-V curves of devices with the structure of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3/PCBM/Ag and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3/PCBM/Ag. (b) Nyquist plots of the SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs and the equivalent circuit model of fitted curves.
Photovoltaic parameters of the SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs under different scanning direction.

head.ce:title: Sandwich-like electron transporting layer to achieve highly efficient perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Passivating the carrier recombination at the heterojunction interface and improving the efficiency of charge separation are effective means to boost the performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The interface modification between the anode and the electron transporting layer (ETL) or constructing bilayer structural ETLs has been proved to be the effective way to achieve high-efficient charge extraction and collection. Combining the advantages of both techniques might further achieve lower energy loss and higher efficiency in PSCs. Herein, we design a sandwich-like SnO2-CQDs-SnO2 (S–C–S) ETLs, i.e. an ultrathin band-gap tunable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) layer is inserted between ultrathin SnO2 bottom layer and SnO2 top layer. The bottom ultrathin SnO2 layer passivates the defects of SnO2:F (FTO) and reduces the carrier recombination at the FTO/ETLs interface. The CQDs layer enhances the optical transmission of ETLs, accelerates carrier transport process and improves the hole-blocking ability. Such S–C–S ETLs greatly enhance the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of PSCs and eliminate hysteresis to the maximum extent. This work provides a new concept for designing novel electronic transmission materials for solar cells, and lays the foundation for further achieving higher PCE in PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite has attracted a worldwide upsurge of research, owing to the remarkable advantages including small exciton energy, tuned band gap, effective ambipolar charge transfer, amazingly high tolerance to defects and strong optical absorption ability [1–13]. The fast increased power conversion efficiency (PCE) in the solar cell based upon organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite indicates its rosy prospect in the field of photovoltaics. In past decades, the PCE of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has been improved from 3.8% to 25.2% via thin-film growth kinetics adjustment, morphology control, interface engineering and device architecture engineering [14–19]. Perovskite materials combine with electron transporting layer (ETL) can construct a planar heterojunction structure [20,21]. For achieving high PCE in PSCs, a suitable ETL should meet the following requirements: (a) good transmittance in the visible light range is necessary to ensure absorbing enough light and reducing the optical energy loss; (b) matching energy level of ETLs with the perovskite materials to reach the expected open-circuit voltage (Voc), which also benefits for improving the efficiency of electron extraction and blocking the holes; (c) higher electron mobility to extract carriers from the absorber in order to reduce charge recombination; (d) in terms of manufacture of PSCs, a high crystal quality ETL film is essential. Based on the above requirements, people normally choose TiO2 and ZnO as ETLs. However, one of big drawbacks of both ZnO and TiO2 ETLs in PSCs is their catalytic activity on perovskite films, which is fatal for the poor stability of PSCs [22]. Moreover, the high temperature fabrication process of TiO2 ETL is disadvantageous for further practical manufacture of PSCs. Recently, owing to its higher electron mobility and low-temperature fabrication technique, SnO2 has emerged as the most hopeful n-type wide bandgap ETL to take the place of TiO2 in planar PSCs [23–29].

Some problems still obstacle the further development of SnO2 based PSCs so far. For instance, to reach the expected open-circuit voltage in PSCs, the energy level of ETLs is required to match well with both perovskite absorber layer and substrates. Whereas, the CBM level (4.03 eV) of SnO2 exhibits big difference with that of the CBM (4.72 eV) of SnO2:F (FTO) [30]. In addition, studies have shown that a compact ETL is an essential element in PSCs for transporting electrons and blocking holes. However, if the ETL is too thick, it may lead to less transmittance and higher series resistance. On the contrary, a thin ETL cannot be able to passivate the defects efficiently so that serious carrier recombination may happen at the interface, which causes the big losses of photo-injected electrons in PSCs during the carrier transport process [31]. Moreover, a quantity of pinholes easily formed in the thin ETL layer can cause shunt pathways and immediate contacts between perovskite and FTO electrode, so that the current leakage may happen, which is harmful to the performance of PSCs. In fact, the thickness of SnO2 film is difficult to control and the compact morphology with high coverage is hard to realize. In consequence, single electron transport layer of SnO2 is not the best choice for PSCs.

Some researchers have tried many methods to turn the single ETL layer into a bilayer [32,33]. Fang et al. introduced the MgO interlayer between the FTO and SnO2 compact layer, which passivated the defects on the FTO and refrained from the leakage due to eliminating the pinholes in the SnO2 film [34]. Kong et al. performed a systematic study on double layer of a-SnO2/c-TiO2 in planar PSCs to improve the carrier transportation [35]. However, no one has yet effectively achieved the goal of both passivating the substrate and improving the carrier transport together by taking this bilayer ETL technique. Thus, the bilayer ETL structure is not the best choice for SnO2-based PSCs either.

Hu et al. and Zhao et al. insert an ultrathin Ag layer between SnO2 layer in inverted perovskite solar cell, which protects both the perovskite and the silver electrode against the detrimental impact of moisture [36,37]. Inspired by the three layers design work, we adopt a novel ETL with sandwich-like structure to solve both problems mentioned above, i.e. we insert an ultrathin Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanolayer as hole-blocking interfacial layer into the SnO2 ETLs. In detail, an ultrathin SnO2 layer was firstly deposited on FTO substrate, and then ultrathin CQDs thin layer and another thicker SnO2 top layer were subsequently deposited to form a kind of SnO2-CQDs-SnO2 (S–C–S) ETLs with sandwich architecture. CQDs are one of promising semiconductors since it displays decent crystallinity and exhibits similar structure like graphene [38–40]. In particular, the CQDs possess tunable band gaps associated with the strong quantum confinement and very obvious edge effects, which facilitates the carrier transport by virtue of boosting of the photocurrent and promoting the electron injection [41,42]. The ultrathin CQDs layer is expected to serve as both modification layer to fill the pin-hole in the ETLs and interfacial layer to block holes, which is helpful for realizing efficient electron extraction, holes blocking and avoiding some leakage caused by pinhole in SnO2. In addition, the coverage of bottom ultrathin SnO2 layer improved smoothness of FTO and decreased its recombination center density to enhance the charge transport. Such “sandwich structure” adopted by us maximize carrier mobility and enlarge free energy difference value to extract electron. Finally, the MAPbI3 PSCs based-on S–C–S ELTs achieves an impressive PCE of 20.78% with almost free of hysteresis. Our results present a new concept to construct ETL with novel integral structure for improving the performance of the PSCs.

Fabrication of S–C–S ETLs. The FTO glass substrates were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone and anhydrous ethanol for 30 min each. After drying, they were treated with Ultraviolet/Ozone for 30 min to remove organic residue on the surface. Subsequently, the SnO2 solutions were spin-coated on the cleaned FTO glass at 8000 rpm for 60 s and then the sample of FTO/SnO2 were annealed at 150 °C for 20 min. Until it cools down to room temperature, CQDs aqueous solution with certain concentration was spin-coated on FTO/SnO2 at 7000 rpm for 30 s with thermal annealing at 100 °C for 5 min in air. After making sure that the water completely evaporates, another SnO2 layer was further spin-coated on FTO/SnO2/CQDs at 7000 rpm for 30 s. After annealing at 150 °C for 20 min, the S–C–S ETLs were successfully prepared.
More details about the preparation process of CQDs and fabrication process of PSCs are demonstrated in Supporting Information.

The scheme image of synthetic process of CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The CQDs were prepared with Monohydrate citric acid (CA) and 1,2,4-Benzeneetriamine dihydrochloride (BD) by one-pot hydrothermal method (experimental details are provided in the Supporting Information) [43]. Fig. 2 a shows the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CQDs. Obviously, the CQDs exhibit uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation. Fig. S1 exhibits the nanoparticle size distribution of as-prepared CQDs, in which the diameter of CQDs can be determined to be in the range of 1.85–7.33 nm. The lattice spacing shown in the HR-TEM image (upper inset in Fig. 2a) is about 0.32 nm, which agrees well with (100) facet of graphite. Moreover, the CQDs can be well dispersed in water as illustrated in inset of Fig. 2b with transparent character.

Fig. 2b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs. The optical band gap of CQDs is identified to be 3.3 eV. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopies (UPS) was used to obtain the relevant conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of CQDs and SnO2 (Fig. S2). The evaluated CB of CQDs is lower than that of SnO2, which is facilitated for the electrons transfers from SnO2 to CQDs. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the CQDs dispersed in water presented in Fig. 2c exhibits an excitation-dependent behavior, which might be due to the uneven distribution of orbital energy of CQDs [44].

Since the deposition of CQDs on the surface of ultrathin SnO2 bottom layer is quite hard to be distinguished by Scanning electron microscope (SEM) technique, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was firstly carried on the FTO/SnO2 and FTO/SnO2/CQDs samples to prove the existence of CQDs. The corresponding N 1s XPS spectra were illustrated in Fig. 3 a. A distinct peak (400.1 eV) assigned to N 1s can be observed in the XPS spectrum of FTO/SnO2/CQDs sample because of the existence of nitrogenous functional groups in CQDs, while the SnO2 bottom layer is lacked of N ligands as indicated in the XPS spectrum of FTO/SnO2 sample. The EDS analysis (Table S1) and the relevant elemental mapping (Fig. S3) of FTO/SnO2/CQDs further indicate the uniform distribution of the respective elements, which reveals that the N and C elements are uniformly distributed on the surface of the ultrathin SnO2 film [45]. Both results certify that the CQDs have succeeded in covering the surface of the ultrathin SnO2 film. Fig. 3b compares the transmission spectra of pristine FTO, FTO/SnO2 and FTO/S–C–S samples. In comparison with other two samples, the FTO/S–C–S exhibits similar transmission in the range of 650–800 nm and much higher transmission in the region of 380–780 nm [46]. When the CQDs ultrathin layer is inserted into both SnO2 layers, the cluster of CQDs might also serves as the light-scattering media to prompt much light transmitting to the perovskite films.

SEM is employed to verify how the ultrathin SnO2 and CQDs layer influence the surface morphology. The top-view SEM image of pristine FTO in Fig. S4a indicated the surface of the FTO is very rough. Ma et al. reported that the rough surface morphology of the substrate has adverse effects on forming dense and smooth perovskite film [34]. After depositing S–C–S layers, the surface morphology turns flatter as shown in Fig. S4b. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is utilized to further characterize the roughness in different FTO/ETLs under standard atmospheric air conditions. According to the AFM images in Fig. 3c–f, the root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of bare FTO, FTO/SnO2, FTO/SnO2/CQDs and FTO/S–C–S is evaluated to be 11, 6.07, 5.93 and 4.83 nm, respectively. That is to say, the S–C–S ETLs with improved coverage and smoothness are obtained after ultrathin SnO2 and CQDs layer modification, which provides a relatively good growth platform for perovskite layer. Usually, the excellent PSCs performance depends on uniform and dense ETL and perovskite absorber layer [47,48]. Therefore, in our case, it is reasonable to believe that the S–C–S ETLs is apt to obtain high performance in PSCs.

The morphology and crystallization of the perovskite films deposited on the pristine SnO2 and S–C–S ETLs are characterized. Fig. 4 a and b illustrate the SEM images of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3. The perovskite coated on S–C–S ETLs expresses nearly full surface coverage with larger grains in comparison with FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 [49]. Fig. 4c exhibits the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3. All existing diffraction peaks indicate the formation of typical tetragonal perovskite structure for both samples. Obviously, the insert of CQDs layer in the SnO2 ETLs has no effect on the crystal structure of perovskite. Compared with SnO2 ETLs, the perovskite films grown on the S–C–S ETLs exhibit the higher diffraction intensity and lower full width half maximum (FWHM), which manifests the best crystallization of perovskite absorber layer [50]. The corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4d) show the similar trend with the results of XRD. We believe the slight enhancement in absorbance is caused by the smoother surface and the higher crystallinity of perovskite films grown on S–C–S ETLs.

The decent electrical properties of ETLs are necessary for achieving high PCE in PSCs. Therefore, the FTO/SnO2/Ag and FTO/S–C–S/Ag architectures are utilized to characterize the conductivity of the ETLs. The linear current-voltage (J-V) curve was employed at 0.3 V bias voltage in dark environments (Fig. S5). The J-V curve of FTO/S–C–S/Ag presents a large slope than that of FTO/SnO2/Ag, which means a more excellent conductivity. The AC Hall measurement with a rotating parallel dipole line magnet system is further performed to acquire the carrier concentration and charge mobility of SnO2 and S–C–S ETLs. It is necessary to extract small Hall voltages with a small signal to noise ratio. Table S2 presents the electrical transport characteristics of different ETLs. In compared with SnO2 ETL, an obvious increase in Hall mobility at structure can be found, which indicates less defects in the S–C–S ETLs [51]. The increase of both conductivity and Hall mobility will reduce the internal resistance and increase the electronic transport performance of PSCs, which will contribute to an improved short-circuit current density (Jsc) and higher PCE.

The PSCs devices are fabricated following the layout structures as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The cross-sectional SEM image of S–C–S-based PSCs is illustrated in Fig. 5b. However, each layer in S–C–S ETLs is too thin for us to distinguish. Before exploring the effect of the S–C–S ETLs on the performance of planar PSCs, we optimize the depositing technique of CQDs layer. The precursor solution concentration of CQDs was varied in the range of 0.05–0.25 mg mL−1 to adjust the thickness of CQDs layer. The corresponding S–C–S films were named as 0.05-CQDs, 0.125-CQDs and 0.25-CQDs. The key photovoltaic parameters of S–C–S based PSCs with different CQDs layer are presented in Table S3, including Voc, Jsc, fill factor (FF) and PCE. With increasing the precursor concentration of CQDs, the PCEs of PSCs firstly enhanced and then went down. We deduce that the 0.05-CQDs film might be too thin to form a dense and compact layer. While, the 0.25-CQDs film obtained from a high concentration solution will be too thick to transport the electrons. Only the precursor concentration of 0.125 mg mL−1 is appropriate to obtain CQDs film with optimized thickness, which is proved to be the optimal condition to produce high-efficiency PSCs. Fig. 5c shows the J-V parameters of the traditional SnO2-based and novel S–C–S based PSCs. Table 1 presents the corresponding photovoltaic parameters. Comparing to SnO2-based PSCs, the S–C–S-based PSCs exhibit higher PCE and weaker hysteresis behavior. In detail, the PCE of S–C–S based PSCs for reverse scan and forward scan is 20.78% and 20.30% respectively. Meanwhile, Fig. S6 shows the steady-state photocurrent density and PCE of the PSCs based on SnO2 and S–C–S ETLs. The steady-state photocurrent density of 23.03 mA cm−2 for the PSCs based on S–C–S ETLs is very close to the Jsc value obtained from the J-V curve, which reveals an excellent irradiation stability of the devices. The hysteresis index “HI” (
H I =
P C
E
F orward
− P C
E
Re v e r s e
P C
E
Re v e r s e
) has been calculated to be only 1.6% for S–C–S based PSCs, which is mainly due to an excellent electron transfer at the interface of S–C–S ETLs and perovskite. All these results proved that, comparing to SnO2 ETLs, the S–C–S ETLs is indeed much more beneficial for enhancing the photovoltaic performance of PSCs.

Fig. 5d further illustrates a histogram of PCE parameters from over 40 devices fabricated on the SnO2 and S–C–S ETLs, obviously indicating that the devices based on the “sandwich structure” exhibits a higher average PCE than those on the pristine SnO2. After analyzing the photovoltaic parameters in Table 1, it is no hard to attribute such PCE enhancement to the increase of Jsc. The integrated photocurrent value of SnO2 and S–C–S based PSCs induced from the external quantum efficiency (EQE) is 19.2 and 22.5 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5e), respectively, which further confirms the variation tendency of Jsc in J-V curves. Base on the above detailed characterization on ETLs and perovskite films, a significantly enhanced absorption in the visible regions caused by the CQDs inserting layer, better morphologies of perovskite layer and higher electron mobility of S–C–S ETLs are undoubtedly the essential factors to increase the Jsc. However, the photo-generated charges separation and transport efficiency is an even more important factor to determine the Jsc value. Fig. 5f illustrates the energy levels scheme of the PSCs. The UV–vis absorption spectra and UPS spectra in Fig. 2b and Fig. S2 are utilized to determine the energy level of CQDs. Normally, the relative electron injection gradually increases with increasing change in free energy (ΔG) [52]. Obviously, the LUMO level of CQDs is far lower than the VBM of SnO2, so that the ΔG of S–C–S based PSCs is larger than that of SnO2 based PSCs, which is beneficial for accelerating the charge transfer to increase Jsc. Meanwhile, such band alignment caused by introducing S–C–S ETL with “sandwich-like” architecture is also beneficial to improve the Voc of PSCs as indicated in Table 1, [35]. As a result, higher PCEs have been achieved in S–C–S PSCs.

Before investigating the effects of S–C–S ETLs on the charge separation and transportation of PSCs, the improved hole-blocking process should be discussed in the aspect of architecture of S–C–S ETLs. As showed in Fig. 6 a, the perovskite covered on the traditional SnO2 ETL may permeate through the pinholes between the SnO2 grains. That must trigger immediate contact between perovskite and anode substrate, which causes severe current leakage and carrier recombination at the interface. After introducing S–C–S ETLs as shown in Fig. 6b, the CQDs can fill the crevice of SnO2 films based on the size difference of CQDs and SnO2 (Fig. 2a and Fig. S7), which makes S–C–S ETLs turn flatter and compact. The CQDs film and ultrathin SnO2 film can play a blocking role to efficiently inhibit the penetration of perovskite to the FTO surface via the pinholes of SnO2. In consequence, it minimizes the possibility of current leakage and carrier recombination.

To investigate the charge separation and transportation of S–C–S based PSCs at interface between ETLs and perovskite, the steady-state PL spectra of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 samples are presented in Fig. 6c. The FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 exhibited a valid PL quenching behavior in comparison with FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3. Moreover, the time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra for the FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 samples are shown in Fig. 6d, which are well fitted via a bi-exponential decay function (1).
(1)
y fit = y 0 + A 1 exp(-t/τ1) + A 2 exp(-t/τ2)

Typically, the fast decay time τ1 is assigned as the transportation of photo-generated carriers from the photo-active film to electron-transporting film and the long decay time τ2 is ascribed to the recombination of free charge carriers in radiative channels [53,54]. As for FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3, the PL decay time of τ1 and τ2 is 23.7 ns and 245.5 ns, respectively. The decay time of τ1 and τ2 of FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 decrease to 19.8 ns and 228.3 ns, respectively. Both the obvious PL quenching and shorter lifetime in FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 sample imply the faster electron extraction from the perovskite film to the S–C–S ETLs with less recombination loss. Moreover, the transient photovoltage decay (TPV) curves were also carried out under opencircuit condition to further study the charge transfer dynamics of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3. The normalized TPV decay curves well fitted by a single exponential decay function are presented in Fig. S8. The photovoltage decay time of FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3 sample (2.08 ms) is much longer than that of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 (1.24 ms). Such prolonged electron lifetime indicates the lower recombination rate in FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3, which implies the efficient charge extraction process happens at the interface. Combined the results of PL, TRPL and TPV characterization, we prove that the S–C–S ETLs with sandwich architecture indeed possess the ability to improve the charge separation and hinder the carrier recombination, indicating that the S–C–S is an excellent electron extraction layer for high efficient planar-type PSCs.

The charge collection efficiency of PSCs is mainly depending on the electron mobility and density of trap states of ETLs. The electron-only device configurations of FTO/SnO2/Ag and FTO/S–C–S/Ag are fabricated to estimate electron mobility of pure SnO2 and S–C–S ETLs by the space charge limited current (SCLC) method. Fig. S9 displays the dark J-V characteristics of both devices. The electron mobilities (μe) in the devices are evaluated according to the Mott-Gurney Law by the formula:
(2)
J
S C L C
=
9
8
ε
0
ε
r
μ
e
(
V
2
L
3
)
where J
SCLC is the current density, ε 0 is the permittivity of free space, εr is the relative permittivity of the material, μe is the electron mobility, V is the applied voltage, and L is the thickness of the ETL film. By calculation, μe of S–C–S (4.93 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1) is much higher than that of the pure SnO2 (3.81 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1). The improved μe will give rise to the more rapid electron transport.

We further fabricate the devices of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3/PCBM/Ag and FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3/PCBM/Ag to evaluate the trap-state density by the SCLC method [55]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the linear J-V relation illustrates an ohmic response at the low bias. The square-law region will terminate in a suddenly rising current with the continuously increasing of voltage. We attribute this phenomenon to the trap filling. The trap-state density is determined by the trap-filled voltage as follows:
(3)
N
t
=
2 ε 0 ε
V
T F L
q
L
2
where L is the film thickness, ε is the relative dielectric constant of SnO2, ε
0 represents the vacuum permittivity, V TFL is the initial voltage of the trap-filled limit region and q is the elemental charge. The calculated trap-state density remarkably decrease from 3.8 × 1015 cm−3 of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3 to 2.0 × 1015 cm−3 of FTO/S–C–S/MAPbI3, which is beneficial for the charge transfer. Hence, both the larger μe in S–C–S ETLs and lower trap-state density in perovskite films deposited on S–C–S ETLs contribute to the efficient charge collection to achieve higher Jsc.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further carried on to evaluate the internal resistance, carrier recombination and charge transport kinetics of the integral PSCs based on S–C–S ETLs. The Nyquist plots of the SnO2-based and S–C–S based PSCs obtained in the dark condition are shown in Fig. 7b, which have been well fitted with an equivalent circuit model of three series resistance capacitance circuits as shown in the inset of Fig. 7b. The corresponding fitting parameters of series resistance (Rs), transfer resistance (Rtr) and recombination resistance (Rrec) are presented in Table S4. Wherein, the x-axis intercept of the high frequency curve is defined to be Rs. The high-frequency curve can be ascribed to the charge transport process, from which we can obtain the Rtr. The low-frequency curve is related to the charge recombination process, from which we can obtain the Rrec. Apparently, the S–C–S based PSCs possess the smaller Rtr of 4.924 × 104 Ω and the large Rrec of 1.394 × 107 Ω. In our case, the perovskite/HTL interface is exactly same for both PSCs, so that the Rtr is mainly determined by the ETL/perovskite interface. The smaller value of Rtr and the larger Rrec testifies the efficient charge extraction and less charge recombination in S–C–S based PSCs, which is attributed to not only better crystallization, fewer grain boundaries and less defects in perovskite films, but also the excellent light transmission and faster electron mobility of S–C–S ETLs. All these results confirm that S–C–S ETLs is the desired electron transfer layer for the planar-type PSC.

Table S5 summaries the device performance parameters employing sandwich-like ETLs reported previously. The parameters of S–C–S sandwich-like ETLs based on CQDs in this work are much better than those of the literatures [36,37]. However, the CQDs are much cheaper than those noble or transition metals in aspect of material/fabrication cost. Thus, the S–C–S sandwich-like ETLs designed by our work exhibits promising practical application potential in PSCs or other type solar cells.

In summary, we construct a new architectural ETL, sandwich-like SnO2-CQDs-SnO2 ETLs, i.e. an ultrathin CQDs layer is inserted between ultrathin SnO2 bottom layer and SnO2 top layer. Both the function of each layer and overall performance of such ETLs have been unveiled by systematically electrical and optical properties characterization, which consistently verifies it is a kind of promising ETLs for solving the problems of light absorption limitation, band alignment and interface charge transfer together. As a result, such sandwich-like ETLs indeed boost the photovoltaic performance of planar PSCs. A 20.78% and 20.30% PCE for reverse scan and forward scan has been achieved, respectively, which is attributed to the formation of high compact and crystal-quality perovskite films, improved charge transfer efficiency via effective band construction and efficient hole blocking effect. The PCE we obtained is ahead of most of pure MAPbI3-based planar perovskite solar cells. The insights into the work mechanism of sandwich-like ETLs presented in this work open a new concept for designing novel architectural ETLs to achieve even higher photovoltaic performance in PSCs in the future.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge financial support for this work from National Natural Science Foundation of China of China (Grant Nos. 61775081, 61904066, 11904127 and 61475063), Program for the development of Science and Technology of Jilin province (Item No. 20180519016JH, 20190103039JH and 20180414008GH), the Thirteenth Five-Year Program for Science and Technology of Education Department of Jilin Province (Item No. JJKH20190998KJ, JJKH20190994KJ, JJKH20191021KJ, JJKH20191022KJ, JJKH20191019KJ), and Construction Program for Innovation Research Team of Jilin Normal University (Grant No.201703).
